nativeRange.habitat: Reclusive reef dweller on offshore coral and rocky reefs at depths of roughly 1–25 m; tends to remain close to rockwork and is quick to retreat into crevices when startled.

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator that has been reported to readily accept most prepared aquarium foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, finely chopped meaty seafood, and quality carnivore-formulated marine pellet or flake preparations. Has been widely documented preying on bristleworms in the display, which is often regarded as a useful side effect, but the same predatory behavior extends to small ornamental shrimp. Generally advised to feed 1–2 small varied meals per day.

summary: The Neon Dottyback is a small, electric-blue-and-orange reef fish from the western Indian Ocean and Persian Gulf, generally considered hardy and beginner-accessible but semi-aggressive and territorial. Best kept singly with ample rockwork.
careNotes:
The Neon Dottyback — also widely sold as the Orange Dottyback — is generally considered one of the hardier, more beginner-accessible saltwater fish. It adapts readily to captive foods, tolerates standard reef water parameters, and is available as both wild-caught and captive-bred specimens, with captive-bred individuals now the dominant form in the trade; wild-caught specimens have become rare.

Despite its small size, the Neon Dottyback has been widely reported as semi-aggressive and territorial: it will defend its rockwork against similarly shaped tankmates and slower fish, and aggression toward conspecifics (members of the same species) is severe. Only one specimen should be kept per tank, and it should not be combined with other dottybacks. Adding it last to a community tank is generally advised, so that established tankmates are less likely to be displaced. Reef compatibility is generally considered good with corals and most sessile invertebrates, but the species has been reliably reported to prey on small ornamental shrimp; larger cleaner shrimp and fire shrimp tend to coexist safely.

A tightly sealed lid is generally advised, as Neon Dottybacks have been reported to jump from open tanks when spooked or chased. Plenty of live rock with numerous caves, crevices, and overhangs is important to give the fish a defendable home territory.

The Neon Dottyback is a bi-directional sex changer — meaning an individual can shift between male and female depending on social conditions — an unusual trait among commonly kept aquarium fish.
breedingNotes: The Neon Dottyback has been bred in captivity by commercial aquaculture facilities, and captive-bred specimens now dominate the trade. As with closely related species, the male is generally expected to prepare a cave or crevice and court a female, who deposits an egg ball inside; the male typically guards the clutch until hatching. Home-aquarium breeding is essentially incompatible with a community setup because conspecifics cannot be safely combined in normal-sized tanks. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging due to the small larval size. Bi-directional sex change has been documented in this species, meaning individuals can switch sex depending on social conditions — a trait that may simplify pair formation in a dedicated breeding setup.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed scientific name (Pseudochromis aldabraensis), care level (Easy/beginner), temperament (Semi-Aggressive), reef-safe (Yes), diet (carnivore — varied carnivore-formulated foods, bristleworm predator), max size (4 in / ~10 cm), minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), captive-bred status (very seldom wild-collected), warning that it 'may eat ornamental shrimp' and should be housed with moderately aggressive larger fish. Note: the LiveAquaria page lists family as 'Labridae' which is incorrect for Pseudochromidae; corrected from FishBase. Reached via the /collections/dottybacks index.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), authority (Bauchot-Boutin, 1958), accepted common name 'Orange dottyback', max length 10.0 cm TL, depth 1–25 m, native distribution (Aldabra, Persian Gulf, Gulf of Oman, Pakistan, Sri Lanka), wild temperature range 24–28°C, reclusive reef habit, IUCN Least Concern (2019), and the notable trait that bi-directional sex change has been confirmed for this species.

additional.name: BiomesInABox (Neon Dottyback complete care guide)
additional.notes: Third confirming source for water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), tank minimum (30 gal), max size (~4 in), reef-safety with the small-shrimp caveat, and the jumping behavior that justifies the escapeRisk: high rating and well-fitted lid recommendation. Confirms severe conspecific aggression (cannot be kept with other dottybacks) and lists compatible tankmates (clownfish, dwarf angelfish, tangs, foxface, gobies).

fish.breedingNotes: The Neon Dottyback is a cave-spawning species: the male prepares a hiding site and courts the female, who deposits an egg ball inside the cave that the male typically guards. The species has been bred commercially by aquaculture facilities, and captive-bred specimens now dominate the trade. Bi-directional sex change has been confirmed for this species, which may aid pair formation in dedicated breeding setups. Home-aquarium breeding is impractical in normal community-sized tanks because conspecifics cannot be safely combined.
